# Introduction

## Purpose

This Software Architecture Document (SAD) provides a comprehensive architectural overview of the AI-Agent for Virtualizing Enterprise Operation system. It presents a number of different architectural views to depict different aspects of the system, serving as a communication medium between the software architect, development team, and other project stakeholders.

The primary goal of this document is to capture and convey the significant architectural decisions that have been made for the system, along with the rationale behind those decisions. It is intended to guide the development team throughout the implementation phase and serve as a reference for future maintenance and evolution of the system.

## Scope

This document covers the software architecture of the AI-Agent platform, a web-based enterprise system that enables organizations to create, configure, and manage AI-powered agents to virtualize and automate internal business operations.

The system encompasses the following core functional areas:

- Agent lifecycle management (creation, configuration, activation, and deletion).
- User and role management with subscription-based access control.
- AI pipeline orchestration and integration with large language model (LLM) providers.
- Knowledge base management for agent context and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).
- Monitoring, logging, and usage analytics.

This document does not cover third-party LLM provider internals, infrastructure provisioning procedures, or front-end design specifications beyond what is architecturally relevant.

## Definitions, Acronyms, and Abbreviations

| **Term**     | **Definition**                                                                                                                  |
| ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| SAD          | Software Architecture Document                                                                                                  |
| AI           | Artificial Intelligence                                                                                                         |
| LLM          | Large Language Model - a deep learning model trained on large text corpora, used to generate or process natural language        |
| Agent        | An AI-powered entity configured within the platform to perform specific automated tasks on behalf of users                      |
| RAG          | Retrieval-Augmented Generation - a technique that enhances LLM responses by retrieving relevant documents from a knowledge base |
| API          | Application Programming Interface                                                                                               |
| SRS          | Software Requirements Specification                                                                                             |
| UC           | Use Case                                                                                                                        |
| RBAC         | Role-Based Access Control                                                                                                       |
| Vector DB    | A database optimized for storing and querying high-dimensional vector embeddings                                                |
| Pipeline     | A configured sequence of processing steps that an Agent executes when handling a user request                                   |
| Subscription | A tiered access plan that governs the features and resource limits available to a user or organization                          |

## References

| **Document**                               | **Description**                                                               |
| ------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Use Case Description Document (PA1)        | Detailed functional descriptions of all identified use cases                  |
| Software Requirements Specification (SRS)  | Functional and non-functional requirements of the system                      |
| Use Case Diagram - Agent Management Module | Visual representation of actors and use cases for the Agent Management module |
| Project Proposal                           | Initial project scope, objectives, and high-level system description          |

## Overview

The remainder of this document is organized as follows:

Section 2 describes the architectural representation style adopted in this document, including the set of views used to capture different perspectives of the system.

Section 3 outlines the architectural goals derived from system requirements and the constraints that have influenced key architectural decisions.

Section 4 presents the Use Case View, highlighting the architecturally significant use cases that have shaped the structure and behavior of the system.

Sections 5 through 9 describe the Logical, Process, Development, Deployment, and Data views of the architecture respectively, each providing diagrams and narrative explanations relevant to that perspective.

# Architectural Representations

This document describes the software architecture of the AI-Agent for Virtualizing Enterprise Operation system using the 4+1 Architectural View Model, originally proposed by Philippe Kruchten. This model organizes the architecture into five complementary views, each addressing the concerns of a distinct group of stakeholders. Together, these views provide a complete and coherent picture of the system without any single view being sufficient on its own.

The five views adopted in this document are as follows:

- Use Case View: describes architecturally significant use cases and actors.
- Logical View: describes the main domain entities, layers, and relationships.
- Process View: describes runtime behavior and interaction flows.
- Development View: describes package organization and dependency structure.
- Physical View: describes deployment infrastructure and runtime environment.

This representation was chosen because the 4+1 model aligns naturally with the UML-based modeling approach adopted throughout the project, and because it provides sufficient separation of concerns to address the varied needs of the project's stakeholders, from business analysts who primarily engage with the Use Case View, to developers who rely on the Logical and Development Views, to operations personnel who reference the Physical View during deployment and maintenance.

# Architectural Goals & Constraints

## Architectural Goals

The architecture is designed with the following goals:

- Scalability: support increasing numbers of users, agents, and AI requests.
- Reliability: maintain stable operation despite partial failures.
- Performance: provide responsive user interactions and efficient request handling.
- Security: enforce RBAC, input validation, and secure communication.
- Maintainability: support easier modification and long-term evolution.
- Extensibility: allow integration of new AI providers and features with minimal changes.

## Architectural Constraints

The architecture operates under the following constraints:

- Backend must use Java Spring Boot and frontend must use ReactJS.
- PostgreSQL is required for relational storage.
- A vector database is required for RAG functionality.
- Subscription-based feature restrictions must be enforced at the backend service layer.
- The system depends on external LLM providers for AI inference.
- The project is developed within an academic semester timeline by a small team.

# Use case view

## Overview

The Agent Management module involves three actors operating under a role inheritance hierarchy. The Admin holds the highest privilege level and inherits the capabilities of the Editor / Member, who in turn inherits from the Viewer. This inheritance model drives the RBAC enforcement strategy applied uniformly across the service layer.

## Architecturally Significant Use Cases

Not all use cases exert equal influence on architectural decisions. The following use cases have been identified as architecturally significant based on their complexity, cross-cutting concerns, or impact on non-functional requirements.

**UC-3.3 - Create New Agent**

This use case involves coordinated operations between the database and the workspace file system. The architecture must ensure consistency between agent persistence and workspace directory creation while also enforcing subscription quota validation.

**UC-3.4 - Edit Agent**

Editing an agent may require both database updates and workspace directory renaming. The architecture applies compensating rollback actions if file system operations fail.

**UC-3.5 - Enable / Disable Agent**

This use case requires immediate UI synchronization with backend state changes while maintaining reliable error handling for failed updates. The toggle interaction must be handled in a way that prevents the UI from entering an inconsistent state in the event of a network or server error, driving a requirement for explicit state revert on the client side when a request fails.

**UC-3.6 - Delete Agent**

Deleting an agent removes both the database record and the associated workspace directory and all its contents. This operation is restricted to the Admin role and requires confirmation before execution, establishing a pattern for irreversible destructive operations throughout the system.

**UC-3.1 and UC-3.2 - View Agent List and View Agent Details**

These use cases define the module's primary read-only access flow and establish the baseline data retrieval behavior for all user roles.

## Actor Summary

| **Actor**       | **Type**             | **Accessible Use Cases**                               |
| --------------- | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Admin           | Primary - privileged | UC-3.1, UC-3.2, UC-3.3, UC-3.4, UC-3.5, UC-3.6         |
| Editor / Member | Primary - standard   | UC-3.1, UC-3.2, UC-3.3, UC-3.4, UC-3.5                 |
| Viewer          | Primary - read-only  | UC-3.1, UC-3.2                                         |
| System          | Supporting           | Participates in all use cases as the backend processor |

## Use Case Diagram

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The diagram illustrates the actor hierarchy, use case associations, and the following relationships:

- An **«include»** relationship from UC-3.1 to UC-3.2, reflecting that viewing agent details presupposes a completed agent list retrieval.
- **«extend»** relationships from UC-3.3, UC-3.4, UC-3.5, and UC-3.6 to their respective extension points: subscription quota enforcement, subscription requirement for edit operations, subscription requirement for toggle operations, and pre-deletion confirmation.
- A **role inheritance** chain: Admin inherits from Editor / Member, who inherits from Viewer.

## Traceability to Functional Requirements

The following table maps each architecturally significant use case to its corresponding functional requirement, as defined in the SRS.

| **Use Case ID** | **Use Case Name**      | **FR ID**      | **Functional Requirement**                       |
| --------------- | ---------------------- | -------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| UC-3.1          | View Agent List        | FR-3.1         | View Agent List                                  |
| UC-3.2          | View Agent Details     | FR-3.1, FR-3.3 | View Agent List; View Agent Details & Edit Agent |
| UC-3.3          | Create New Agent       | FR-3.2         | Create New Agent                                 |
| UC-3.4          | Edit Agent             | FR-3.3         | View Agent Details & Edit Agent                  |
| UC-3.5          | Enable / Disable Agent | FR-3.4         | Enable / Disable Agent                           |
| UC-3.6          | Delete Agent           | FR-3.5         | Delete Agent                                     |

# Logical View

## Overview

The Logical View describes the key domain entities, their relationships, and the internal organization of the system from a functional perspective. For the Agent Management module, the logical structure is organized around three layers: the Presentation Layer (REST controllers), the Application Layer (services), and the Data Layer (repositories and entities). This layered approach reflects standard Spring Boot conventions and keeps the codebase straightforward to implement and maintain within the scope of this project.

## Domain Model

The central entity in this module is the Agent, which represents an AI-powered virtual employee configured within a workspace. The Agent entity holds the following attributes:

- id: unique identifier (UUID).
- name: display name, unique within the workspace.
- role: the agent's job title or function.
- model: the AI language model assigned to the agent.
- instruction: behavioral guidance written to the agent's skill.md file.
- status: enumeration of ACTIVE or INACTIVE.
- createdAt: timestamp of creation.
- updatedAt: timestamp of last modification.

The Agent entity maintains a many-to-one relationship with Workspace, meaning one workspace may contain multiple agents, while each agent belongs to exactly one workspace. The Workspace entity itself is managed by a separate module; within this module it is treated as a read-only dependency used for quota validation and directory path resolution.

## Package Structure

The module is organized into the following packages:

- **controller:** Contains the AgentController class, which exposes the REST API endpoints for all agent operations. The controller is responsible for receiving HTTP requests, delegating to the service layer, and returning appropriate responses. It does not contain any business logic.
- **service:** Contains the AgentService class, which acts as the central coordinator for all agent operations. This class encapsulates the business logic of the module, including input validation, subscription quota checks, database writes, and file system operations. By consolidating these responsibilities behind a single service interface, AgentService functions as a Facade, shielding the controller from the complexity of cross-subsystem coordination.
- **repository:** Contains the AgentRepository interface, which extends Spring Data JPA's JpaRepository to provide standard database access operations. Custom query methods for workspace-scoped lookups and name uniqueness checks are declared here.
- **entity:** Contains the Agent class, annotated as a JPA entity, along with the AgentStatus enumeration (ACTIVE, INACTIVE).
- **dto:** Contains the data transfer objects used at the API boundary. AgentRequest carries the input fields submitted by the user (name, role, model, instruction). AgentResponse carries the data returned to the client, derived from the Agent entity. This separation ensures that internal entity structure is not directly exposed through the API.
- **infrastructure:** Contains the WorkspaceFileService class, responsible for all file system interactions: creating the agent directory, writing skill.md, renaming the directory on agent rename, and deleting the directory on agent deletion. Isolating file system logic in a dedicated class makes it independently testable and replaceable without touching business logic.

## Class diagram

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The class diagram illustrates the layered interaction between the controller, service, repository, entity, DTO, and infrastructure components of the Agent Management module.

AgentController depends exclusively on AgentService, preserving the separation between the presentation layer and lower-level concerns.

AgentService acts as the orchestration component coordinating repository access and workspace file operations through WorkspaceFileService.

DTO classes (AgentRequest and AgentResponse) isolate the API contract from the internal persistence model represented by the Agent entity.

## Applied Design Patterns

**Repository Pattern**

All database access is mediated through the AgentRepository interface. The service layer never issues queries directly; it always goes through the repository. This decouples business logic from persistence concerns and makes it straightforward to swap or mock the data access layer during testing.

**Facade Pattern**

AgentService presents a clean, simple interface to the controller layer while internally coordinating multiple operations: validating subscription limits, persisting the agent record, and invoking WorkspaceFileService for file system changes. From the controller's perspective, creating an agent is a single method call. The complexity of ensuring consistency across the database and the file system is entirely contained within the service.

**DTO Pattern**

Input and output data are represented by dedicated DTO classes rather than exposing the Agent entity directly at the API boundary. This prevents unintended exposure of persistence-layer details, simplifies input validation using Spring's validation annotations, and allows the API contract to evolve independently of the internal domain model.

# Process View

## Overview

The Process View describes the dynamic behavior of the Agent Management module at runtime - how the system responds to user actions, how operations are sequenced across layers, and how the system handles failures. Each flow described in this section corresponds to one or more architecturally significant use cases identified in Section 4.

All operations in this module are executed synchronously within the HTTP request-response cycle. The Spring Boot application handles each incoming request on a dedicated thread from the servlet container's thread pool. No background jobs, message queues, or asynchronous processing are involved in the Agent Management module, keeping the runtime model straightforward and predictable.

## Create New Agent (UC-3.3)

This is the most complex flow in the module due to the need for coordinated writes across two subsystems: the relational database and the workspace file system.

Flow description:

- The user submits the agent creation form. The HTTP request is received by AgentController, which delegates immediately to AgentService.
- AgentService validates the request input: name format and required fields.
- AgentService queries the Subscription Service to retrieve the current agent count and the plan limit for the workspace. If the limit has been reached, the service throws a business exception and the controller returns an appropriate error response to the client. No further processing occurs.
- AgentService checks AgentRepository for an existing agent with the same name within the workspace. If a conflict is found, a validation exception is thrown.
- AgentService persists the new Agent record to the database via AgentRepository with status ACTIVE.
- AgentService invokes WorkspaceFileService to provision the agent directory and write the skill.md file.
- If the file system operation fails, AgentService deletes the previously persisted Agent record (compensating transaction), restoring the system to its pre-operation state. An error response is returned to the client.
- If all steps succeed, AgentController returns the newly created agent's details to the client.

Key architectural point: Because the database and the file system are two separate resources that cannot participate in a single atomic transaction, the rollback in step 7 is implemented as an explicit compensating action rather than a transactional rollback. This is a deliberate design decision driven by the absence of distributed transaction support across heterogeneous storage systems, as discussed in the Data Consistency Strategy in Section 9.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_UC-3.3 Sequence Diagram_

## Edit Agent (UC-3.4)

This flow shares the cross-subsystem coordination concern with UC-3.3, with the additional complexity of a conditional directory rename.

Flow description:

- The user submits the updated agent configuration. AgentController delegates to AgentService.
- AgentService validates the updated fields. If the name has changed, a uniqueness check is performed against AgentRepository.
- AgentService updates the Agent record in the database via AgentRepository.
- If the agent name was changed, AgentService invokes WorkspaceFileService to rename the workspace directory from the old name to the new name.
- If the directory rename fails, AgentService rolls back the database update by restoring the original agent name and configuration. An error notification is returned to the client.
- AgentService overwrites the skill.md file with the updated instruction content via WorkspaceFileService.
- On success, AgentController returns a confirmation response to the client.

Key architectural point: The rename operation in step 4 is only triggered when the name field has actually changed. If only non-name fields are modified (role, model, instruction), the directory is left untouched and only the database record and skill.md are updated, simplifying the operation and reducing the risk of unnecessary file system errors.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_UC-3.4 Sequence Diagram_

## Enable / Disable Agent (UC-3.5)

This is the simplest write operation in the module, but it is architecturally notable because of the requirement for immediate and consistent UI state reflection.

Flow description:

- The user clicks the status toggle. AgentController receives the request and delegates to AgentService.
- AgentService retrieves the current agent record and determines the target status (ACTIVE → INACTIVE or INACTIVE → ACTIVE).
- AgentService updates the agent status in the database via AgentRepository.
- On success, AgentController returns the updated status to the client. The UI reflects the new state immediately.
- If the database update fails, AgentController returns an error response. The client is responsible for reverting the toggle to its previous visual state, ensuring the UI remains consistent with the actual persisted state.

Key architectural point: No file system interaction occurs in this flow. The skill.md file and the agent directory are unaffected by a status change. This keeps the toggle operation lightweight and low-risk.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_UC-3.5 Sequence Diagram_

## Concurrency and Threading Model

The Agent Management module operates entirely within Spring Boot's default synchronous servlet model. Each HTTP request is handled on a single thread for its entire duration. There are no background threads, scheduled tasks, or asynchronous operations within this module.

Concurrent requests targeting the same agent. For example, two users attempting to rename the same agent simultaneously, are handled at the database level through JPA's default optimistic locking behavior. No explicit locking strategy is applied within the application layer for this module, which is an accepted trade-off given the low concurrency expectations of the academic deployment context.

# Development View

## Overview

The Development View describes the static organization of the Agent Management module's source code from a developer's perspective. It defines how the codebase is divided into layers and packages, what each package is responsible for, and how dependencies flow between them. This view serves as a guide for developers during implementation, ensuring that the layered structure established in the Logical View is consistently reflected in the actual code organization.

## Layer Architecture

The module follows a three-layer architecture:

- Presentation Layer: handles HTTP requests and responses through AgentController.
- Application Layer: contains business logic and workflow coordination through AgentService and WorkspaceFileService.
- Data Layer: contains entities and repositories for database access.

Dependencies flow strictly from upper layers to lower layers.

## Module Responsibilities Summary

The following table summarizes each package, its primary class or interface, and its responsibility within the module.

| **Package**    | **Primary Class / Interface** | **Responsibility**                                                                             |
| -------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| controller     | AgentController               | Expose REST endpoints; delegate to service layer; return HTTP responses.                       |
| service        | AgentService                  | Enforce business rules; coordinate database and file system operations; handle error recovery. |
| infrastructure | WorkspaceFileService          | Create, rename, and delete agent directories and skill.md files on the workspace file system.  |
| repository     | AgentRepository               | Provide database access operations for the Agent entity via Spring Data JPA.                   |
| entity         | Agent, AgentStatus            | Define the JPA-mapped domain entity and its status enumeration.                                |
| dto            | AgentRequest, AgentResponse   | Carry input data into and output data out of the service layer at the API boundary.            |

## Dependency Rules and Rationale

Three dependency rules are enforced throughout the module and should be respected during implementation.

- The first rule is that the controller package must not import anything from the repository, entity, or infrastructure packages directly. All access to those concerns must go through the service layer. This ensures that business logic cannot be bypassed at the API level.
- The second rule is that the infrastructure package must not import from the repository or dto packages. WorkspaceFileService is concerned exclusively with the file system; it should have no awareness of the database or the API contract.
- The third rule is that the entity package must not import from any other internal package. Domain entities are passive data structures and must remain free of application logic and external dependencies.

# Physical/Deployment View

## Overview

The Physical / Deployment View describes how the software components of the Agent Management module are deployed onto the runtime infrastructure. It illustrates the mapping between the application's logical components and the physical environment in which they execute, including application servers, databases, storage systems, and external services.

The deployment architecture follows a simplified cloud-oriented structure suitable for the project's academic scope while remaining consistent with production-oriented architectural practices. The system is designed as a web-based client-server application with clear separation between the frontend, backend, persistence layer, and external AI services.

## Deployment Architecture

The system is deployed using a multi-tier architecture composed of the following primary nodes:

- **Client Node (Web Browser):** The frontend application is executed within the user's web browser. It is implemented using ReactJS and communicates exclusively with the backend through HTTPS REST API calls. No direct database or file system access is permitted from the client side.
- **Application Server:** The backend system is deployed as a Spring Boot application running inside a Docker container. This server hosts all core business logic, including agent management, RBAC enforcement, subscription validation, and workspace file coordination.

The application server exposes REST endpoints consumed by the frontend and acts as the central coordinator between the database, file system, and external AI services.

- **PostgreSQL Database Server:** Relational application data is stored in a PostgreSQL database. This includes:
  - Agent metadata.
  - Workspace information.
  - User accounts and roles.
  - Subscription records.
  - Audit timestamps and configuration data.

The database server is isolated from direct external access and is reachable only from the backend application server.

- **Workspace File Storage:** The workspace file system stores physical agent resources, including the agent's dedicated directory, the skill.md behavioral configuration file, uploaded reference documents, and other workspace artifacts. This storage is accessed exclusively by the backend application server through the WorkspaceFileService component and is not exposed to the client directly.
- **Vector Database:** A dedicated vector database stores embedding vectors used for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). The backend communicates with the vector database during document indexing and semantic retrieval operations.

This component is logically separated from PostgreSQL because vector similarity search workloads differ significantly from relational query workloads.

- **External LLM Provider:** The system integrates with third-party LLM providers through HTTPS APIs. These providers perform AI inference for agent responses and pipeline execution.

Because the providers are external services outside the system boundary, the architecture treats them as potentially unreliable dependencies and isolates all interactions behind service abstractions.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_Deployment diagram_

# Data View

## Overview

The system uses multiple storage technologies depending on the type of data being managed:

- PostgreSQL stores relational application data.
- A vector database stores semantic embeddings for RAG.
- The file system stores workspace resources and uploaded files.

This separation improves scalability and maintainability.

## Primary Data Stores

**PostgreSQL Relational Database**

PostgreSQL serves as the system's primary transactional data store. It manages all structured application data that requires consistency, relationships, and transactional integrity.

The following information is stored in PostgreSQL:

- Agent metadata.
- Workspace information.
- User accounts.
- Role assignments.
- Subscription plans and limits.
- Audit timestamps.
- Configuration settings.

Relational storage is appropriate for these entities because they involve strong relationships, validation rules, and frequent transactional updates.

**Vector Database**

The vector database stores high-dimensional embedding vectors used by the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) subsystem.

The following data is stored:

- Document embeddings.
- Chunk metadata.
- Semantic search indexes.
- Retrieval references linked to workspace resources.

Unlike relational queries, semantic similarity search requires efficient nearest-neighbor vector operations. For this reason, embeddings are isolated from the relational database and managed through a specialized vector storage system.

**Workspace File Storage**

The file system stores unstructured workspace artifacts associated with agents.

Examples include:

- skill.md files.
- Uploaded reference documents.
- Temporary processing files.
- Agent workspace directories.

The file system is used instead of relational storage because these resources are document-oriented and may vary significantly in size and format.

## Core Data Entities

**Agent**

The Agent entity represents an AI-powered virtual employee configured within a workspace.

Key attributes include:

- Id.
- name.
- role.
- model.
- instruction.
- status.
- createdAt.
- updatedAt.
- workspaceId.

Each Agent belongs to exactly one Workspace.

**Workspace**

The Workspace entity represents an organizational environment that groups users, agents, and resources together.

Key responsibilities include:

- Ownership boundary.
- Subscription association.
- Agent grouping.
- File system namespace resolution.

A single Workspace may contain multiple Agents.

**User**

The User entity represents a platform user authenticated within the system.

Key attributes include:

- Id.
- email.
- passwordHash.
- role.
- subscriptionId.
- workspaceId.

Users interact with the system according to their assigned RBAC permissions.

**Subscription**

The Subscription entity defines feature limits and access permissions for a workspace or user account.

Examples of controlled limits:

- Maximum number of agents.
- Access to premium models.
- Advanced pipeline capabilities.
- Workspace storage limits.

Subscription data is consulted before executing privileged operations such as agent creation or editing.

## Entity Relationships

The primary relationships in the Agent Management module are as follows:

- _One_ Workspace can contain _many_ Agents.
- _One_ Workspace can contain _many_ Users.
- _One_ Subscription can govern _multiple_ Workspaces depending on business rules.
- _One_ Agent belongs to exactly _one_ Workspace.
- _One_ User belongs to exactly _one_ Workspace.

These relationships are enforced through foreign key constraints at the relational database level.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_Entity Relationships Diagram_

## Data Flow

The Data View covers three primary data flows within the Agent Management module. During agent creation, the frontend submits a request to the backend API; the backend validates subscription limits against PostgreSQL, persists the agent record, and provisions the workspace directory and skill.md file on the file system. During agent retrieval, the backend queries PostgreSQL and returns the agent list or detail to the frontend; no file system access is required for read operations. During agent deletion, the backend removes the database record and subsequently deletes the corresponding workspace directory; if the file system deletion fails, the database record is restored through a compensating action.

## Data Consistency Strategy

The system applies different consistency strategies depending on the storage type involved.

**Relational Consistency**

Operations involving only PostgreSQL rely on standard transactional guarantees provided by Spring Transaction Management and PostgreSQL ACID compliance.

Examples include:

- Agent status updates.
- User role modifications.
- Subscription updates.

**Cross-Resource Consistency**

Operations involving both the database and the file system cannot participate in a single distributed transaction.

Instead, the architecture applies compensating actions:

- If database persistence succeeds but file creation fails, the database record is explicitly removed.
- If a directory rename fails during an update operation, the database changes are reverted.

This approach provides practical consistency without introducing the complexity of distributed transaction coordinators.

**Vector Data Consistency**

Embedding generation and vector insertion are treated as secondary operations relative to core transactional data.

If vector indexing fails:

- The primary relational transaction remains valid.
- The system may retry indexing later.
- The agent itself remains operational even if semantic retrieval features are temporarily incomplete.

This trade-off prioritizes system availability and operational resilience.
